goals.description:
All MUSD students will receive standards-based instruction, curriculum, and assessments by highly
qualified teachers in inclusive, culturally responsive environments to be fully prepared for college
and career. Unduplicated students (low socio-economic, Foster/Homeless Youth, English Learners)
as well as Students with Disabilities will make accelerated growth.

actions.title:
General Education
Staffing
actions.description:
Continue to recruit, hire, and retain a diverse group of highly qualified
teachers that reflect our community of learners and are skilled at creating
powerful learning environments, establish strong relationships with
students, standards-based daily instruction, and partnership with families
for student success. Continue partnership with institutes of higher
education for teacher residency and other programs aimed at developing a
diverse teacher pipelines (such as SJSU Men of Color, SJSU Residency in
Special Education, SCCOE LEAP program for special education
credentials, including those that provide an education pathway for our
classified employees).

actions.title:
Special Education
Staffing
actions.description:
Continue to recruit, hire and retain a diverse group of staff to provide high-
quality Special Education services for identified students that reflect our
community of learners and are skilled at creating powerful learning
environments, establish strong relationships with students, standards-
based daily instruction, and partnership with families for student success.
Grow inclusion programming, specifically at the secondary level through
co-teaching. Continue partnership with institutes of higher education for
teacher residency and other programs aimed at developing a diverse
teacher pipelines (such as SJSU Residency in Special Education and
SCCOE LEAP program for special education credentials, including those
that provide an education pathway for our classified employees).

actions.title:
Support and
Administrative
Staffing
actions.description:
Continue to recruit, hire and retain a diverse group of site support staff and
administrators to support rigorous instruction and preparation of all
students for college and career readiness that reflect our community of
learners and are skilled at creating powerful learning environments,
establish strong relationships with students, standards-based daily
instruction, and partnership with families for student success.
LREBG Funded Action: MMCHS secretary, MMCHS principal, MHCs,
Intervention Coordinator, L and D Director
Evidence Based Practice: Middle college programs are a strong research-
based practice because they significantly improve college readiness for
low-income students by providing them with early exposure to college-level
coursework and environments through dual enrollment. This structured
approach, coupled with strong academic and social supports and/or
interventions, leads to higher rates of high school graduation, college
enrollment, persistence, and degree completion, particularly for students
who are traditionally underrepresented in higher education.
Attendance interventions like district School Attendance Review Board
(SARB) processes are strong research-based practices because they
employ a multi-tiered system of support that addresses absenteeism from
prevention to intensive intervention. This comprehensive approach, often
involving collaboration between schools, families, and community
agencies, aims to identify and address the root causes of non-attendance,
thereby improving student engagement and academic outcomes.
An intervention coordinator is crucial for MTSS because they serve as a
system-builder, organizing and aligning the various tiers of support,
resources, and data-driven decision-making processes across a school or
district. Research indicates their role ensures fidelity of implementation for
evidence-based interventions, providing leadership and coaching to staff,
which ultimately maximizes student achievement and well-being.
Metrics: attendance and chronic absenteeism, SBAC ELA and Math, A-G
Completion Rate, and Dual Enrollment
Funds: $580,000

actions.title: High Impact Tutoring
actions.description:
C o mprehensive System of High Impact Tutoring (90 minutes weekly) for
students in TK-10 who are one of more grade levels behind:
target TK-2 low-income and Foster/Homeless Youth for in person, play-
based small group tutoring. (Please note: this part of the action is also part
of our Coordinated Intervening Services (CCEIS) action plan which is
designed to reduce over identification of Latinx and Black students in
special education.)
target Hispanic English Learners for in person, ELD small group tutoring
target 3-6 grade (inclusive of SWD) for virtual, small group math/ELA
tutoring
target 9th and 10th graders for math (inclusive of SWD)
For foster youth/homeless students provide transportation and food in
order for them to access services.
LREBG Funded Action: High Impact Tutoring Vendor Contract
Evidence Based Practice: High-impact tutoring is a strong research-based
practice due to its consistent demonstration of significant learning gains for
students, often accelerating students academic progress by several
months. This effectiveness is attributed to key characteristics such as
frequent, personalized instruction from trained tutors, strong student-tutor
relationships, and alignment with the school curriculum, all supported by
robust data analysis.
Metrics: iReady Assessment
Funds: $850,00

actions.description:
Develop, refine, and grow April Acceleration Academy, Newcomer

Learners, low-income, and foster/homeless youth to accelerate their

Dashboard prioritize student recruitment and enrollment in April

actions.title:
Technology Tools
and Blended
Learning
actions.description:
Continue online learning pathways, 7-12 EducatEverywhere Virtual
Pathways Program, and intervention/enrichment programs as well as
investigate new tools to address the personalized needs of English
Learners, low-income, and foster/homeless youth in order to build core
skills and access the general curriculum. Technology tools will also be
targeted to meet the needs of LTELs in grades 4-12.
Continue Goalbook to improve CCSS aligned IEP goals and regular
monitoring of student progress.
Investigate and purchase VR tool(s) to fundamentally change the learning
experience for students, specifically English Learners and low-income
students.
Investigate and purchase AI tutoring tool(s) to address the personalized
needs of English Learners, low-income, and foster/homeless youth in order
to build core skills and access the general curriculum.

Development:
Administrators
actions.description:

Data Meetings in alignment with district priorities. Develop a special

actions.title:
Adult Professional
Development: TK-6
Teachers
actions.description:
A districtwide professional development calendar will be developed and
maintained to prevent scheduling conflicts and support sites with adequate
substitute coverage.
P-3 Alignment: Provide substitute coverage for staff to participate in P-3
Alignment. Content will focus on standards alignment in math and Science
of Reading with specific strategies to serve English Learner, students with
disabilities as well as general education students to build a strong
foundation for all subject areas.
Grade 4-6 Alignment: Provide resources and stipends for professional
development time on science of reading practices. Assessment tools that
align with Science of Reading and math.
Grade 1-3 STEAM Teachers: Provide substitute coverage for staff to
participate in professional development with a content focus on creating
access, specifically for English Learner and Special Education through
integrated supplemental instruction in the areas of math, science, and art.
Four full days and monthly after school meetings.
Grade 4-6 Science Teachers: Provide substitute coverage for staff to
participate in professional development with a content focus on creating
access, specifically for English Learner and Special Education. Four full
days and monthly after school meetings.
General Education and Special Education Teachers: provide professional
development on inclusion best practices.

actions.title:
Adult Professional
Development: 7-12
Teachers
actions.description:
A districtwide professional development calendar will be developed and
maintained to prevent scheduling conflicts and support sites with adequate
substitute coverage.
Deeper Learning Assessments: Provide substitutes for release time for
staff to participate in Deeper Learning assessment design training to
address challenges with student academic outcomes and improve
students’ engagement and learning experience. Content of training will
include a specific focus on serving Latinx, Black, and Students with
Disabilities.
AI Literacy: Provide stipends for staff to participate in AI Literacy training so
that we continue to elevate our curriculum and instruction. Content of
training will include a specific focus on serving English Learners, Students
with Disabilities, and low-income students.
General Education and Special Education Teachers: provide professional
development on inclusion best practices, specifically co-teaching models.

actions.title:
English Learner
Development
actions.description:
English Learner Practices: shift the focus of the District EL TOSA primarily
to secondary schools and LTELs. This shift allows more personalized
support to secondary schools for one 1:1: job-embedded professional
development. Content will focus on supporting English Learners in the
classroom, specifically increasing student to student talk time while
keeping the rigor in lessons. Provide coaching for staff and ELD teacher
staffing.
Leverage Elementary Intervention TOSAs to provide in person intervention
for EL students (4th-6th graders/LTELs) and CCEIS target students (K-2).
In addition, to direct student support TOSAs and PFELDs will support
classroom teachers through the lesson study process to research and
implement content specific student talk structures as well as train our
special education teachers on the reclassification process for dually
identified students to directly support our English Learners and address
areas of Technical Assistance.
LREBG Funded Action: Intervention TOSAs
Evidence Based Practice: Lesson study is a strong research-based
practice for teacher professional development because it fosters
collaborative inquiry among teachers, allowing them to collectively plan,
observe, analyze, and refine actual classroom lessons. This cyclical
process directly links professional learning to student outcomes, as
teachers gather and reflect on data about how students learn and respond
to instruction, leading to continuous improvement in teaching practices.
Metrics: Reclassification Rate
Funds: $100,000

actions.title:

and General
Education Co-
Teacher
Development
actions.description:
Inclusive Practices Elementary: leverage the Inclusion TOSA provide job-
embedded professional development for general education and special
education teachers on inclusive practices to address challenges for
students with disabilities to access to LRE. Content of training will include
a specific focus on serving Students with Disabilities in the general
education setting in the areas of differentiation ELA and math.
Spangler, Pomeroy, and Zanker Elementary Schools will be prioritized first
for coaching sessions in ELA, special education.
Zanker Elementary will be prioritized first for coaching sessions in math,
special education.
Inclusive Practices Secondary: Add a secondary Inclusion TOSA role to
provide job embedded professional development. The target groups will
be co-teaching partners (classified and/or certificated staff). Content will
include a specific focus on serving secondary special education students in
the general education setting to address challenges with student access to
LRE in the areas of math and ELA and; thereby, increase in the number of
special education students graduating college and career ready.
Rancho Middle School will be prioritized first for coaching sessions.
MHS will be prioritized for coaching sessions.

actions.title:
Newcomer Welcome
Team
actions.description:
Provide specific trauma-informed supports for newcomer students and
families to support their transition and engagement with school.
Year 1: Establish a Family/Newcomer Center by leveraging and organizing
existing structures and personnel to be more intentional (i.e. Randall
Family Resource Center and community liaisons) to welcome newcomer
students and families. Work with school-based teams to evaluate student
wellness. Develop an orientation workshop.
Homeless families across the district and at Rose and Spangler will be
prioritized for support.
Low-income families across the district and Spangler, Weller, Zanker,
Russell will be prioritized for support.
Year 2: Evaluate the program need and effectiveness. Refine based on
feedback. Determine next steps.

actions.title: Healthy Snacks

Youth and families to engage in High Impact Tutoring and after school
programming.

actions.title: Transportation
actions.description:

means to come to school and families to engage in school and access
school related services (i.e. High Impact Tutoring/other after school
programming)

actions.title:
Mental Health
Systems
actions.description:
Investigate and purchase an Electronic Health Record (EHR) system by
June 2026 and begin billing for behavioral health services by fall 2026.
Join the SCCOE cohort for the CYBHI grant to develop private insurance
billing strategies to expand mental health services to students and families.
Use Keenan to support suicide prevention. Continue to provide Care
Solace as a mental health resource for students, families and staff to
provide low-income, Foster Youth/Homeless and SWD with basic mental
health services so they are better able to access their learning.

actions.title:
Mental Health
Staffing
actions.description:
Transition MHS Wellness Center staff (Mental Health School Wellness
Specialist and Wellness Center Liaison) funding from Santa Clara County
Office of Education to the MUSD budget in order to support student and
staff mental health needs. The current partnership will continue in the
2024-25 school year, MUSD will investigate funding options to continue its
implementation.
Increase ratio of mental health providers (school counselors, school
psychologists, and school therapist (those who carry MFT, LPCC, MSW,
etc.) ) to align with the nationally recommended targets (e.g. school
counselors at 1:250, school psychologists at 1:500, school social workers
1:300, etc.). During the 25-26 year reintroduce the mental health clinicians
intern program as well as add partnerships to provide direct services to
students off-site.
Further define and refine the Mental Health provider roles and how
services are blended (school psychologists, Mental Health counselors,
SLS Coordinator and SLS Supervisor, school counselors, community
liaisons, school nurses and health clerks, and program manager for Mental
Health) to provide access to basic health and mental health needs and
resources to ensure students remain health and cared for thus, increasing
attendance.
Further define and refine the Mental Health provider roles and how
services are blended (school psychologists, Mental Health counselors,
SLS Coordinator and SLS Supervisor, school counselors, community
liaisons, school nurses and health clerks, and program manager for Mental
Health) to provide access to basic health and mental health needs and
resources to ensure students remain health and cared for thus, increasing
attendance.
LREBG Funded Action: Mental Health Clinician Staffing and Secondary
School Counselors
Evidence Based Practice: Hiring school-based mental health clinicians is a
strong research-based practice because it significantly increases student
access to crucial mental health services in an accessible and less
stigmatizing environment. Research consistently shows that this leads to
improved student well-being, reduced mental health symptoms, and better
academic outcomes, including decreased self-reported suicide attempts
and improved academic and psychosocial functioning. Together our
Counselors and Mental Health Clinicians provide Tier 1-3 social emotional
behavioral training and support.
Metrics: Attendance
Funds: $1.4 million

actions.title:
Administrative and
Support Staff Staffing
actions.description:
Maintain full time Elementary and Secondary Assistant Principals across
s it e s in order to support the culture and climate (restorative practices and
attendance) needs of low-income, English Learner, and Homeless/Foster
Youth students.
Maintain full time School Links Coordinator and School Links Supervisor of
Wellbeing and Immigrant Support to meet the needs of ALL students, with
a focus on low-income, English Learner, and Homeless/Foster Youth
students.
Maintain full time health clerks across our elementary sites to ensure
student wellbeing by meeting the physical needs of students during the
school day and engaging families to support positive attendance, with a
focus on low-income, English Learner, and Homeless/Foster Youth
students.
These actions are principally directed towards our English learners,
Foster/Homeless Youth, and low income students.

Social Emotional and
Mental Health
Supports

Site leaders will implement MTSS Tier 1-3 Social Emotional and Mental
Health supports aligned to Positive Behavioral Intervention Supports
(PBIS) and Restorative Practices.

actions.title:
Diversity, Equity, and
Inclusion
actions.description:
The Culture of We Equity Leaders will guide, develop, and sustain equity
initiatives in order to disrupt racism and improve all school and site
systems for our community. The MUSD Equity Team operates as a
transitional, intermediary organization within the structures of the district,
actively partnering with all to build the will and skill of the district to
actualize educational equity for all of our scholars.
Expose how embedded, systemic white supremacist values in education
are both upheld and/or dismantled.
Apply an antiracism lens when reviewing current policies or creating new
ones.
Transform systems to create equitable outcomes for all marginalized youth
and families.
Cultivate and sustain equity within the MUSD community.
Promote shared accountability in the necessity and urgency of equity and
anti-racism.

actions.title:

Support Staff Staffing
actions.description:
Maintain full time community liaisons to support culturally responsive,
i n c l usive, and timely communication with English Learner families.

actions.title:
Parent Education
Workshops and
Events
actions.description:
Provide Parent/Guardian Workshops and training in partnership with
parents. Content will include behavior routines, academics, and supporting
early learners (TK/K) with transitions, transition for elementary to middle
school and middle to high school. The content will focus on students with
disabilities and foster/homeless youth to increase supporting access to
general education and student engagement, sense of belonging and
increase student attendance.
Build community awareness of MUSD programming for incoming
preschoolers and Transition Kindergarten age students in order to close
the opportunity gap and/or provide early interventions as appropriate.
School sites will host family engagement events to promote meaningful,
personalized, and positive family engagement opportunities to build
community and ensure student success.

actions.title:
Parent and Students
Events to Foster the
MUSD Culture of We
actions.description:
School sites and MUSD will host culturally responsive family events to
create a sense of belonging for our most marginalized students by
p r o viding opportunities for gathering and connecting among affinity groups.
Events will be selected to reflect diverse cultures and some in partnership
with the City of Milpitas, including but not limited to Green Scholars, Black
to School, and community recognitions (i.e. Flame Keepers and
Horizontes). Focus groups will include students with disabilities as well as
African American and Latinx ethnicities to increase a sense of belonging
and student attendance.
During the 2025-2026 year, develop action steps tied to the problem
statement developed by the Community Design Team: Students, families,
and staff across our diverse school community want to feel connected,
understood, respected, and heard; however, current school structures,
practices, and communication patterns often prioritize transactional
interactions over authentic relationships. These missed opportunities for
constructive connection between students and staff, among peers, and
across school and family partnerships undermine collaboration, belonging,
and the support needed for all students to thrive. Additionally, build the
Community Design process into a Milpitas practice.
